title: The Chinese Eunuch Who Burned a Fleet
event_summary: Following the death of the Xuande Emperor, Confucian officials in the Ming court, led by Vice President of the Ministry of War Liu Daxia, ordered the destruction or suppression of Zheng He's detailed navigational charts and records of his seven massive voyages across the Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, and East Africa. The treasure fleet — comprising ships that dwarfed anything Europe would build for another century — was left to rot in Nanjing's dockyards. China's most advanced oceangoing capability was deliberately dismantled within a single generation.
why_forgotten: The destruction was so thorough that it succeeded on its own terms: later Chinese historians had little material to work with, and the voyages became a footnote rather than a turning point. Western historiography, focused on European Age of Exploration narratives, had no incentive to highlight that China had explored first and then voluntarily withdrew. The event was an absence — the deliberate non-happening of Chinese global expansion — which is harder to remember than action.
why_it_matters: Had China maintained its naval supremacy, the Portuguese, Spanish, and Dutch would have encountered a dominant Chinese maritime presence across the Indian Ocean, potentially preventing European colonial empires from forming as they did. The decision to turn inward reshaped the entire global balance of power for centuries, enabling Europe's unchallenged expansion into Asia and Africa. Modern China's Belt and Road Initiative is explicitly framed by Chinese scholars as a resumption of Zheng He's maritime legacy — making this 600-year-old bureaucratic decision a living force in contemporary geopolitics.
